title: BOSS BATTLE — Deep Signal
narrativeIntro:
A transmission arrives from the deepest part of this region. It's broken — the data pipeline that once carried it has decayed. Promises fail silently. Types are mismatched. The async keyword is missing where it matters most. The builders left this system unfinished, or perhaps time broke it.

You have 45 minutes. Trace the data from source to destination. Fix every broken link. Make the signal complete.
narrativeOutro:
The signal arrives. Complete, intact, decoded. A deep tone resonates through the second cluster, and every node you activated this week brightens in response. The two clusters — week one and week two — are now connected by a single, luminous thread.

The third region is different. It's not code fragments and functions. It's architecture. Components, services, systems that compose into something larger than their parts. This is where understanding becomes building.
phmConnectionNote: A major turning point — Ryland and Rocky face their biggest challenge yet. You face yours.
conceptsPreview: Boss battle — debug a broken multi-file async data pipeline

challenges.title: Deep Signal
challenges.narrativeHook: Three failures, one pipeline. The signal exists. You just have to make it through.
challenges.instructions:
The signal pipeline is broken in exactly three places. `pipeline.ts` is the only file you can edit — `types.ts` and `api.ts` are read-only.

Trace the data from `fetchSignal()` to the final `PipelineResult`. Fix every failure. All three tests must pass.

You have 45 minutes.

files.name: types.ts
api.ts
pipeline.ts

tests.description: processSignal(1) should decode the signal and return { ok: true, signal: { message: 'HELLO' } }
tests.testCode: const r1 = await processSignal(1); if (!r1.ok) throw new Error(`processSignal(1) should succeed, got error: ${r1.error}`); if (r1.signal.message !== 'HELLO') throw new Error(`Expected message 'HELLO', got '${r1.signal.message}'`);

const r2 = await processSignal(1); if (!r2.ok) throw new Error(`processSignal(1) failed: ${r2.error}`); if (r2.signal.receivedAt !== '2024-01-01T00:00:00.000Z') throw new Error(`Expected receivedAt '2024-01-01T00:00:00.000Z', got '${r2.signal.receivedAt}'`);

tests.description: processSignal(0) should return { ok: false, error: 'Invalid signal ID' }
tests.testCode: const r3 = await processSignal(0); if (r3.ok) throw new Error('processSignal(0) should fail'); if (r3.error !== 'Invalid signal ID') throw new Error(`Expected error 'Invalid signal ID', got '${r3.error}'`);

explainGate.prompt: Describe each of the three bugs you fixed and how TypeScript's type system would have caught at least one of them before the code ran.

hints: The first failure prevents the code from running at all. Look for a keyword that's required when using `await` inside a function.
`decodeHex()` expects a string of hex characters. Check the type of `signal.id` versus `signal.raw` — TypeScript's interface in types.ts tells you which is which.
JavaScript `Date` objects are constructed with milliseconds since the Unix epoch. The `timestamp` field in the Signal interface is in seconds.
